summary: O'Neil documents her experience knitting a pussyhat during Trump's inauguration and discovering that the knitting community had organically mobilized for political activism. She describes seeing pink yarn and pussyhats everywhere—at Vogue Knitting Live, in yarn stores, on planes heading to D.C., and proliferating on Ravelry. The post celebrates how craft communities can become unexpected sites of political resistance, channeling creative energy into collective action.
key_points: The pussyhat project transformed a solitary craft into a visible form of collective political protest
Knitting communities proved naturally suited for activism due to their collaborative, generous culture
The phenomenon spread rapidly across physical spaces (yarn stores, planes) and online platforms (Ravelry)
Crafting and activism aren't separate activities—the same people doing one are often doing the other
money_quotes: I thought I was going to enjoy the juxtaposition of activist-to-insane hobbiest, but I was wrong – knitters were activists too!
Now that it's happened, it's obvious that knitters are a perfect community for activism.
We're friendly, community-oriented, and desperate for an opportunity to make something and give it away.
Because it gives us an excuse to buy more yarn.
It took less than two hours because I was using super bulky yarn and because I had lots of anxious energy to tap into.
key_insight: Craft communities like knitters are natural activists because their culture already values generosity, community, and creating things with purpose.
